Eligibility Criteria 
1.  
i. 
The agency must be registered under the Societies Registration Act, Companies Act, Limited Liability Partnership (LLP) under the 
Partnership Act, Trust Act etc. The agency/s must have Income Tax Registration (PAN) and GST Registration. Consortium/ Joint 
Venture is not allowed. 
ii. 
The Agency should have an experience of at least five 5 years in Agri & allied sector 
 
Grant Thornton India LLP is a Limited Liability Partnership Firm registered in India, having registration 
number AAA 7677. Grant Thornton is providing advisory services in the country from more than 20 years. It 
was registered on 19th May, 2000 as a partnership firm (Partnership Deed attached as Annexure). Later in 
January 2012 it converted to a Limited Liability Partnership Firm. Grant Thornton is engaged with several states 
and federal government agencies providing its sectoral expertise in developing and strengthening agriculture, 
food processing industry and agribusiness infrastructure. During this period, the firm has more than 16 years of 
experience in Agro-Food Industrial Infrastructure, Agriculture & allied sector with various Central/State 
Government/PSU’s and Multilateral/Bilateral agencies for creating the infrastructure across the country.

A- Profile of Agency/Organization 
 
About Grant Thornton International 
 
Global Firm with Presence Across Continents 
 
Grant Thornton globally is a network of independent assurance, tax and 
advisory firms, made up of 56,000+ people in 140 countries 
 
Our global footprint is significant, being present in all major business centers of the world. Firm global 
penetration is increasing rapidly, but we still like to concentrate on the depth of our service delivery, 
not breadth. Towards that end, our member firms endeavor to enhance and grow our service line 
capabilities by attracting brilliant minds and offering truly distinctive client service. We are capable of 
bringing you quality service wherever in the world you are or want to be, via our large number of 
member firms. 
 
Our global footprint in a snapshot

About Grant Thornton India LLP 
 
Grant Thornton India LLP is part of the Grant Thornton global network of independent assurance, tax and 
advisory firms. Proactive teams led by approachable partners in these firms, use insights, experience and 
instinct to understand complex issues for privately owned, publicly listed and public sector clients and 
help them to find solutions.  
 
The firm was incorporated on 19th May 2000 vide Registration No. AAA-7677 under section 58(1) 
of the Limited Liability Partnership Act, 2008 and is one of the largest management advisory firms in 
India. With over 4,500 professional staff across 15 offices, the firm provides robust compliance services 
and growth navigation solutions on complex business and financial matters through focused practice 
groups. The firm has extensive experience across a range of industries, market segments, and 
geographical region. It is on a fast-track to becoming the best growth advisor to dynamic Indian 
businesses with global ambitions. With shorter decision-making chains, more senior personnel 
involvement, and empowered client service teams, the firm is able to operate in a coordinated way and 
respond with agility.  
 
Our extensive network of our member firms and offices in the country can help mitigate the challenges 
arising on account of the unique legal system, regulatory bottlenecks and tax requirements complemented 
by strategy and policy advisory needs.  
 
The firm helps in financial and business growth by overcoming these barriers of growth. This involves 
advisory, corporate finance, management of your business risks, strategic planning of your investments 
and solving your taxation related queries. 
 
Over the years, GT has also added lateral talent across service lines and has developed a host of 
specialist services in areas such as MSME cluster development, Private Sector and Entrepreneurship 
Development, Social Development, Agriculture, Education & Skills, Monitoring and Evaluation, Public 
Private Partnerships, Urban and Municipal Infrastructure, Industrial Infrastructure, Transport, e-
Governance, Sports Infrastructure, Procurement Advisory, Project Management etc.  
 
Our Expertise

Experience in Agriculture Business Advisory 
 
GT has a dedicated agribusiness advisory division managed by a team of more than 300 experts that 
bring in diverse capabilities across the value chain of the sector. The team has experts specializing in 
areas such as farming techniques, crop cultivation, irrigation expertise, development of farm produce

Development Program of National Horticulture Board 
 Technical Proposal  18 
 
 
We have assisted number of farmer producer organisations by market linkages with retail chains, 
exporters, processors, etc. for marketing their produce. We facilitate financial linkages to prospective 
agriculture entrepreneurs and commodity based organizations (CBOs) with Banks/Financial Institutions 
in availing credit for Project. Some of the key projects are: 
 
 
Consultancy Services to Function as Agri-Business Promotion Facility of Rajasthan Agricultural 
Competitiveness Project 
 
Consultancy Services to Function as Agri-Business Promotion Facility of Maharashtra 
Agricultural Competitiveness Project 
 
Project Management Agency (PMA) for four Mega Food Parks under the Mega Food Parks 
Scheme of Govt. of India, Ministry of Food Processing Industries (MoFPI), Government of India 
 
State of Maharashtra’s Agribusiness and Rural Transformation (SMART) Project- Involved in 
preparation of 30 pilot Productive Partnership Plans, Collateral Management Agency Intervention 
Plans and Market Access Plans with a total Project Cost of above 300 crores with 31 target crops. 
The project involves multiple stakeholders right from private sector to the Community Based 
Organisations (CBOs) like FPCs, PACs etc.  
 
Consultancy Services for “Formation of Industry Associations (IAs) & Implementation of Agro 
Industrial Development Plans (AIDPs): Cluster Development Technical Agency (CDTA) 
 
For various schemes of Ministry of Food Processing Industries (MoFPI), Government of India we 
have been playing the role of Program Management Agency and Project Management Consultants for 
the and have facilitated 100s of cold chain, food park and food processing projects. It may be highlighted 
that the firm has assisted in financial closure of more than USD 180 Millions in multiple food processing 
projects. Key project are listed below: 
 
 
Project Management Agency (PMA) for four Mega Food Parks under the Mega Food Parks Scheme 
of Govt. of India, Ministry of Food Processing Industries (MoFPI), Government of India 
 
Project Management Agency (PMA) for Scheme for Cold Chain, Value Addition and Preservation 
Infrastructure, Ministry of Food Processing Industries (MoFPI), Government of India 
 
For various schemes/projects of Ministry of Food Processing Industries (MoFPI), Government of 
India we have been evaluating detailed project reports (DPRs) and reviewing the viability of the project if 
it is sustainable for grants release. Key project are listed below: 
 
Project Management Agency (PMA) for four Mega Food Parks under the Mega Food Parks Scheme 
of Govt. of India, Ministry of Food Processing Industries (MoFPI), Government of India
pages.20:
Technical Proposal: Engagement of an Agency/Organization for Setting up a Program Management Unit for Cluster 
Development Program of National Horticulture Board 
 Technical Proposal  19 
 
Project Management Agency (PMA) for Scheme for Cold Chain, Value Addition and Preservation 
Infrastructure, Ministry of Food Processing Industries (MoFPI), Government of India 
 
Capacity building is an integral part of all our engagements. We have been organizing capacity 
building workshops, training for Board of Directors (BoDs) and other key members on various aspects 
in the sector related to entrepreneurship, multiple aspects within agri value chain from farm to fork and 
other such knowledge sharing activities. Some of our notable assignments with the World Bank on these 
lines include: 
 
 
Consultancy Services to Function as Agri-Business Promotion Facility of Maharashtra 
Agricultural Competitiveness Project 
 
Consultancy Services for Formation, Strengthening & Business Plan Development of Farmer 
Producer Organizations/Companies in Andhra Pradesh 
 
Consultancy Services to Function as Agri-Business Promotion Facility of Rajasthan Agricultural 
Competitiveness Project 
 
We provide incubations services and mentoring to the farmer groups with the objective to identify, 
mobilize and groom prospective agriculture entrepreneurs and commodity-based organizations (CBOs). 
We also specialise in post-investment incubation services for Agri-business entrepreneurs. Some of the 
key projects are: 
 
 
Consultancy Services for Formation, Strengthening & Business Plan Development of Farmer 
Producer Organizations/Companies in Andhra Pradesh 
 
Consultancy Services to Function as Agri-Business Promotion Facility of Maharashtra 
Agricultural Competitiveness Project 
 
Consultancy Services to Function as Agri-Business Promotion Facility for the State of Punjab 
 
Consultancy Services to Function as Agri-Business Promotion Facility of Rajasthan Agricultural 
Competitiveness Project 
 
We have assisted several private sector clients in appraising the Detailed Project Reports (DPRs) 
including review of cost estimates, reasonable estimation of the likely revenues, preparation of cash flow 
statements and the revenue model indicating financial viability, commercial sustainability and socio-
economic impact for according final approval to the projects for grant release under various schemes. 
Notably, the firm has been working with over 500 FPCs and has directly evolved more than 60 
FPCs in the recent past. 
 
Thought leadership in Agri-Business Sector 
 
At Grant Thornton, our people provide insights on a variety of topics that not only relate to the success 
and sustenance of businesses but also reach a wider audience consisting of policy makers and 
consumers. 
 
We publish reports, newsletters, research studies and white papers on monthly and quarterly bases. 
Through our diverse publications, we seek to not only broaden our own knowledge base but also 
contribute to the knowledge society at large. We are aware that as thought leaders with specialised 
knowledge and industry expertise, our actions are likely to inform public policy, a responsibility that, like 
everything else, we shoulder with great care. Our views/comments on policies impacting the public are 
much sought after and have affected changes to those that are antithetical to public interest. 
 
GT has recently published two books released at the highest level and also used for training FPOs and 
Agri-business start-ups. 
 
 
“Agripreneur start-ups: Manual with operation guidelines for promotion of agri-business 
enterprises” published by MACP and authorised by Prof. V. Padmanand, Sushil Khodwekar, 
Kunal Sood and Chetan Bhakkad, September 2018 
 
“Manual on intrapreneurship and Management of FPCs” published by MACP and authorised by 
Prof. V. Padmanand, Sushil Khodwekar, Kunal Sood, 2018

Narrative description of the Project: 
The client has launched the scheme for Creation of 
Backward & Forward Linkages in the country with the 
objective of providing integrated & complete creation of 
Backward & Forward Linkages facilities without any 
break from farm gate to the consumer. 
 
The client has issued the guidelines effective from 14th 
July 2017 for implementation of scheme & has decided 
to engage Programme Management Agency (PMA) to 
assist the Ministry in implementation of scheme in 
general & appraisal, monitoring & implementation of the 
projects assisted under the above scheme in particular 
& provide management, capacity building, coordination 
& monitoring support through the entire project.  
 
Grant Thornton India LLP has been appointed as a 
Project Management Agency (PMA) for the said scheme

by your staff within the assignment:

indicating the financial viability, commercial 
sustainability & socio-economics impact & 
presentation of the appraisal to Ministry for 
approval

Scrutiny of the proposals received against the 
EOI & assist the Ministry in selection of eligible 
proposals

Narrative description of the Project: 
Grant Thornton is responsible for refining the project 
design, conducting due diligence, and assessing project 
feasibility in various aspects required by ADB and the 
government. GT will hold two tripartite workshops to 
introduce the team’s findings, discuss issues, and set 
directions for way forward. 
Description of actual services provided 
by your staff within the assignment: 
Grant Thornton India has to set up a Programme 
Management Unit for assisting the Government of 
Maharashtra and Asian Development Bank (ADB) in the 
preparation of this loan project. We are refining the 
project concept and design of MAGNET by incorporating 
international best practices and innovative technologies 
that may fit the local context in Maharashtra. As the main 
beneficiaries (farmer’s organizations and agriculture 
value chain operators) are in the private sector, 
sufficient involvement of and consultation with private 
stakeholders is the most critical aspect to make the 
project design relevant to the local context. Therefore, 
as part of our engagement, GT India is conducting 
multiple stakeholder workshops and consultations with 
such participants across 10 different horticulture value 
chains. The main purpose of these workshops is to 
understand the constraints and issues faced by various 
FPOs and value chain operators both in terms of the 
supply chain and marketing infrastructure. These 
workshops therefore play a crucial role in the overall 
design of the project and in terms of meeting the 
objective 
of 
improvement 
in 
productivity 
and 
enhancement of capacities of FPOs and value chain 
operators thereby benefiting horticulture farmers:

This CONTRACT (together with the General Conditions, Appendices A-E, 
and Notice to Proceed as an integral part, called the Contract) is made on the 16th day of 
December 2019, between Asian Development Bank (ADB), and Grant Thornton India LLP, 
India [CMS No. 024332] on the other part, (as the Consultant). Although the Consultant has 
associated with Palladium Consulting India Private Limited, India [CMS No. 032076], the 
Consultant retains full and undivided responsibility for performing the obligations and 
completing satisfactorily the Services under the Contract. 
ADB has agreed to assist the Government of India (the Government) with technical 
assistance (Technical Assistance) for the TA-9738 IND: Strengthening Capacity to Design and 
Implement Water and Rural Infrastructure Facility - Preparing the Maharashtra 
Agribusiness Network Project (53145-001) by an agreement (the Technical Assistance 
Agreement) executed between the Government and ADB on the 18th day of June 2019; and 
ADB has requested the Consultant to work under the terms and conditions below, 
and under the General Conditions and the Appendices as attached to which the Consultant has 
agreed; 
THEREFORE the parties agree as follows: 
Clause 1.   Description of Services. The Consultant's work under the Contract (the 
Services) is detailed in the Terms of Reference in Appendix A. 
Clause 2. Reports. The Consultant submits to ADB in English the reports specified 
below and in Section 6.05 of the General Conditions:

copy to INRM) and 3 copies to the Government] and to be submitted within
four (4) weeks after commencing the Services.

Terms of Reference 
Selection 154635: TA-9738 IND: Strengthening Capacity to Design and Implement Water 
and Rural Infrastructure Facility 
—Preparing the Maharashtra Agribusiness Network Project (53145-001) 
1.
Terms of Reference Key Words
Horticulture, postharvest, marketing, agriculture value chain, farmers organization, rural 
development, financial intermediation loan, financial intermediaries 
2.
Objective and Purpose of the Assignment
1.
ADB will engage a consulting firm in accordance with ADB Procurement Policy and
Procurement Regulations for ADB Borrowers (2017, as amended from time to time) and its
associated project administration instructions and staff instructions. A total of 8.0 person-months
of international and 25.0 person-months of national consultant inputs are expected. The selection
will follow a quality-and cost-based selection (QCBS) with a quality-cost ratio of 90:10 with biodata
technical proposal. Areas of expertise required will include (i) horticulture agribusiness and
marketing, (ii) agriculture value chain and logistics facility (iii) information technology, (iv) financial
intervention design and due diligence, (v) finance management assessment and financial analysis,
(vi) agricultural economics, (vii) procurement, (viii) social development and safeguards, (ix)
environmental safeguard, (x) climate change and disaster risk management.
2.
Objective and purpose. The consulting firm will help the government and ADB in the
preparation of a loan project to be approved in 2020. The consulting team will refine the project
concept and design described in the concept paper1 by incorporating international best practices
and innovative technologies fit to the local context in Maharashtra, India. The team will conduct
technical, safeguards, economic, financial, and other due diligence to meet government’s and
ADB’s project processing requirements. As the main beneficiaries (farmers organizations and
agriculture value chain operators) are in the private sector, sufficient involvement of and
consultation with private stakeholders will be critically important to make the project design
relevant to the local context. The consultants should also learn lessons and incorporate the inputs
from ADB’s past projects (e.g. MFF 0045-IND, Grant 9147-IND), pre-feasibility study (“mapping
study”), and ongoing technical assistances (e.g. TA 9057-REG).2
3.
Scope of work. The consulting firm will be responsible for refining the project design,
conducting due diligence, and assessing project feasibility in various aspects required by ADB
and the government. The firm is expected to hold two tripartite workshops to introduce the team’s
findings, discuss issues, and set directions for way forward. The workshops should be attended
by various stakeholders including relevant civil society organizations and private operators in
addition to the executing agency, ADB mission, and the consulting team. The firm will deliver the
following outputs:
(i)
inputs to ADB’s Report and Recommendation of the President (especially data and
facts required for the rationale, design and monitoring framework indicators
(baseline data), and other sections);
1 The concept paper is under processing as of 7 October 2019, and its draft is shared with the consultants as reference. 
2 The reports are also shared with the shortlisted firms comprising: mapping study final report and TA Report for TA 
9057-REG.

4. 
Tasks and Qualification Requirements 
 
A. International Consultants 
 
5. 
Horticulture agribusiness and marketing specialist/team leader (international, 2.0 
person-months) 
(i) 
Detailed tasks and/or expected output 
The consultant will be responsible for overall scope of the transaction technical 
assistance (TRTA) as the team leader and specifically for designing the scope of 
capacity development (output 1) and financial support (output 2). 
 
The consultant will: 
a. prepare a horticulture agribusiness and marketing strategy building upon the 
Mapping Study, TA 9057-REG (ongoing), the World Bank’s SMART project 
(ongoing), and other relevant studies and projects; 
b. identify the gaps and needs to be addressed in the project including the needs 
for capacity development (output 1), financial support (output 2), and value 
chain infrastructure development (output 3); 
c. lead organization of workshops and meetings for the TRTA; 
d. lead the consultant team to facilitate ADB missions; 
e. lead planning of TRTA implementation; 
f. lead coordination with concerned government agencies and development 
partners; and 
g. compile outputs from all the consultant team members to prepare all the TRTA 
consultant outputs and reports; and 
h. respond to any other TRTA assignments which arises during the 
implementation. 
 
(ii) 
Minimum qualification requirements 
a. Required qualification: degree in agriculture, agricultural economics, or a 
relevant discipline  
b. Minimum general experience: 20 years 
c. Minimum specific experience (relevant to assignment): 15 years 
d. Regional/country experience: required 
 
6. 
Agriculture value chain and logistics facility specialist (international, 1.5 person-
months) 
(i) 
Detailed tasks and/or expected output 
The consultant will be responsible for designing both the hard (infrastructure) and 
soft (capacity development and financial support) components of the project from 
agriculture value chain and logistics perspectives, especially for designing the 
scope of the value chain infrastructure (output 3) and financial support (output 2). 
 
The consultant will: 
a. conduct value chain and market analyses for the selected crops (banana, 
custard apple, green and red chili, guava, okra, orange, pomegranate, sapota, 
strawberry, and sweet lime) taking the past pre-feasibility study (Mapping 
Study) into consideration;
pages.65:
Appendix A 
 
4 
 
b. prepare a value chain development strategy and identify components to be 
included in the ensuing loan such as capacity development (output 1), financial 
support (output 2), and infrastructure development (output 3); 
c. develop a set of subproject selection criteria for the financial intermediation 
loan component (FIL) based on the value chain analyses and value chain 
development strategy; 
d. prepare the overall feasibility study report together with other specialists; 
e. provide guidance to prepare and improve the detailed project reports (DPRs) 
to be prepared by the government. For the hard infrastructure components, 
optimize the design to maximize the relevance to the current and future market 
demand, to reduce the greenhouse gas emissions, and to adapt to disaster 
risks and climate change impacts; 
f. quantify climate financing amount for both mitigation and adaptation together 
with the climate change and disaster risk mitigation specialists based on 
Guidance Note on Counting Climate Finance at ADB and 
g. provide inputs to draft Report and Recommendation of the President and 
relevant linked documents. 
 
(ii) 
Minimum qualification requirements 
a. Required qualification: degree in agriculture, agricultural economics, logistics, 
or a relevant discipline  
b. Minimum general experience: 15 years 
c. Minimum specific experience (relevant to assignment): 10 years 
d. Regional/country experience: preferred 
 
7. 
Finance specialist (international, 1.5 person-months) 
(i) 
Detailed tasks and/or expected output 
The consultant will be responsible for financial aspects of the overall project and 
design and due diligence of the financial intermediation loan (FIL) component 
under the guidance of ADB’s finance specialists. He/she will work closely with a 
national Financial Intermediation Loan Design Specialist, who will be recruited 
separately through an individual consultant selection (before the firm is engaged). 
 
The consultant will (i) carry out financial management assessment (FMA) of the 
executing and implementing agencies, including (a) assessing whether previous 
financial management assessments have been conducted by ADB or other 
agencies and, if so, reviewing the results and ascertaining whether these can be 
used as input, (b) assessing capacity for planning and budgeting, management 
and financial accounting, reporting, auditing, internal controls, and information 
systems, (c) reviewing proposed disbursement and funds-flow arrangements, and 
(d) concluding on the financial management risk rating and identifying and 
confirming measures for addressing identified deficiencies; (ii) support the 
preparation and agreement of cost estimates and a financing plan, which are 
based on verifiable data and are sufficient to support project implementation; (iii) 
assess and prepare financial projections for the project components in accordance 
with ADB guidelines and carry out financial evaluations (financial cost-benefit 
analyses) including sensitivity analyses for the project over the construction and 
operation period by calculating the financial internal rate of return and comparing 
it with a weighted average cost of capital; (iv) conduct financial analyses of the 
executing and implementing agencies by reviewing the historical financial 
performance and positions and preparing financial projections of the agencies, and
pages.66:
Appendix A 
 
5 
 
incremental recurrent costs, to determine financial sustainability, and reviewing 
proposed cost-recovery and tariff policies, including affordability; (v) develop the 
design of the funds flow, disbursement mechanisms, accounting, auditing, and 
financial reporting arrangements for the ensuing project (including the FIL), based 
on the FMA of executing/implementing agencies; (vi) identify significant risks to 
project financial sustainability and/or viability, and conduct sensitivity analysis on 
risks identified; (vii) propose any further capacity building activities and/or 
covenants (financial management and ADB’s disbursement procedures) that will 
be necessary to mitigate the identified risks; (viii) assess market demand and 
analyze/propose proper amount for the FIL component; (ix) develop Excel-based 
spreadsheet model supporting the financial analyses and evaluation concerned; 
(x) provide relevant inputs for TRTA reports to be consolidated by the team leader; 
(xi) prepare Report and Recommendation of the President (RRP) and linked 
document (Financial Management Assessment and Financial Analysis), and 
provide inputs to relevant sections regarding financial analysis and financial due 
diligence in the RRP and linked documents for the project; and (xii) respond to 
comments on the above documents, and revise and update relevant documents 
necessary to seek ADB Board approval for the loan project by incorporating and 
addressing comments received. 
 
For the FIL component (and a part of the project loan component if appropriate), 
the consultant will be responsible for undertaking a CAMELS (C: capital adequacy, 
A: asset quality, M: management quality, E: earnings, L: liquidity, and S: sensitivity 
to market) review and integrity due diligence on candidate financial intermediaries 
(FIs). The data and information generated by the review will be used to identify 
suitable partners to support ADB intervention in financing support to farmers 
organizations and value chain operators. The work would be accomplished 
through combination of desk research, market knowledge, interviews, consultant 
intellectual capital and corporate investigation. Site visits will also be made. 
Specifically, the consultant will: 
a. work closely with the Team Leader and relevant team members to carry out 
market research and assessments to identify investment needs, and financing 
demands and barriers for the business of farmers organizations and value 
chain operators; 
b. assess market demand and analyze/propose proper amount for the FIL 
component together with the ADB team and the consultants’ Team Leader and 
relevant team members; 
c. develop appropriate financing mechanism and customized financial 
instruments for farmers organizations and value chain operators to address the 
investment needs and financing demands in support of the horticulture 
postharvest and marketing development achieving the project outcome and 
outputs. Such financing mechanisms can include a loan, matching grant 
scheme, savings and credit unit, and/or guarantee fund. These mechanisms 
can be financed not only through an FIL but also through a project loan as 
appropriate; 
d. develop financial management procedures and fund flow arrangements for the 
FIL; 
e. propose pricing strategies and terms for the financial instruments, fund flow 
arrangements and fund management rules, sub-project selection criteria, sub-
project screening, selection, approval, administration procedures under the FIL 
in compliance with relevant regulations and requirements;

f. propose institutional and governance arrangements to ensure fiduciary 
oversight and safeguard compliance for the FIL; 
g. coordinate with the Team Leader and relevant team members to screen and 
identify possible subprojects to be financed by the FIL and in consultation with 
the executing agency and ADB project team to prepare an initial long list of 
eligible SMEs subprojects to be financed by the FIL meeting the ADB financing 
requirements. To enable leveraging and re-utilizing the FIL proceeds, prioritize 
the eligible subprojects in multiple batches (up to 2–3 batches depending on 
the market demand and subproject financing terms) allowing revolving the FIL 
proceeds for financing eligible subprojects after the sub-loan for the first batch 
subprojects is repaid. Finalize the first batch of subprojects to be financed by 
the FIL; 
h. prepare the cost estimates of subprojects in accordance with the requirements

including base costs, physical contingencies, price contingencies, and financial

appropriate and needed; 
i. 
prepare the financing plan for the FIL taking into account the cofinancing and

j. 
prepare contract awards and disbursement projects for the FIL; 
k. prepare accounting, auditing, and financial reporting arrangements for the FIL; 
l. 
prepare detailed FIL implementation guidelines and subproject financing 
agreement templates between the participating FIs and sub-borrowers under 
the FIL. The templates shall reflect the requirements stipulated in Loan

m. carry out financial management assessments for the participating FIs to ensure 
that the participating commercial banks and financial institutions have 
adequate financial management systems and processes in place for the 
implementation of the FIL. The financial management assessments shall 
evaluate staffing, accounting policies and procedures, budgetary framework, 
fund flow arrangements, internal controls, audit systems, information system, 
reporting and monitoring systems, credit policies, systems and procedures, 
investment limit, risk management policies, systems and procedures, 
governance, business plans, and financial management risks for each 
participating FIs. Use ADB Financial Management Assessment Questionnaire 
(FMAQ) as a basic template and reference to collect information. Further 
enhance and augment the FMAQ to complete financial management 
assessments for each participating FIs as required and appropriate to due 
diligence requirements for the FIL. The financial management assessments 
shall identify strength and weakness of each financial management aspect and 
propose improvement measures and action plan to address the weakness 
identified. The financial management assessment shall also include risk 
mitigation measures and actions to address risk identified. Prepare financial 
management assessment reports to document in detail the financial 
management assessments; 
n. conduct financial analysis to assess the financial condition and performance of 
the participating FIs and/or FI guarantors. Analyze the financial statements for 
each participating banks and financial institutions and compute relevant ratios

to determine the profitability, solvency, liquidity, and capital adequacy of the 
entities, including but not limited to debt service coverage ratio, debt equity

determining the appropriateness of an FI includes its profitability, solvency, and

FIL component; and

9. 
Social development and safeguards specialist (international, 1.0 person-months) 
(i) 
Detailed tasks and/or expected output 
The consultant will be responsible for conducting social and gender analysis and 
safeguard due diligence on involuntary resettlement and indigenous people of the 
project components under the guidance of ADB’s social specialists.  
 
The consultant will (i) prepare socioeconomic and poverty profiles for the areas; 
(ii) based on the initial poverty and social assessment, conduct poverty, gender, 
and social analyses in accordance with ADB’s Safeguard Policy Statement (SPS, 
2009) and other relevant guidelines; (iii) assess other social issues including 
poverty, gender, and labor impacts; (iv) prepare a gender action plan (and a social 
development action plan as necessary); (v) identify persons and communities 
potentially affected by land acquisition and resettlement based on ADB guidelines 
in case such any impacts are observed from the project components; (vi) for 
project sites where land acquisition and resettlement has already been 
implemented, review the land acquisition and resettlement reports and ensure that 
the report meets ADB requirements; (vii) conduct gender analysis and prepare a 
gender action plan with detailed information in the areas and potentials to enhance 
economic opportunity through (a) skilled jobs and training opportunities in the 
project related activities, (b) targeted support to women-owned/led businesses, 
and (c) increased benefit from the project outputs; (viii) collect sex-disaggregated 
data (quantitative and qualitative) to inform the gender designs and provide the 
baselines or proxy data for gender targets in the DMF and Gender Action Plan; (ix) 
prepare inputs to (a) the Report and Recommendation of the President to the 
Board of Directors (RRP) (b) relevant sections in Project Administration Manual, 
(c) Gender Actin Plan, (d) Summary Poverty Reduction and Social Strategy, (e) 
Risk Assessment and Risk Management Plan, and (f) Financial Intermediary: 
Environmental and Social Management System Arrangement (for the FIL below); 
(x) assist ADB project team to respond to comments received during ADB internal 
review on the deliverables produced above, and (xi) finalize the deliverables by 
incorporating and addressing the comments received. 
 
For the financial intermediation loan (FIL) component, the consultant will cooperate 
closely with the environmental safeguard specialists and conduct the following 
tasks: (i) collect and compile data and information necessary for due diligence of  
financial intermediaries (FIs) to assess capacity and commitment of each of the 
FIs to manage involuntary resettlement and indigenous peoples impacts in 
accordance with the ADB’s SPS and national legislation, (ii) prepare environment 
and social management system (ESMS) arrangement to assist FIs to establish 
ESMS, and (iii) organize and conduct training on social safeguards for FIs.

The consultant will be responsible for conducting climate risk and vulnerability 
assessment (CRVA) and environmental safeguard due diligence of the project 
components under the guidance of ADB’s environment specialists.  
 
The consultant will (i) collect information and data required and prepare IEE 
including environmental management plans (EMPs) for all infrastructure 
subprojects; (ii) undertake the climate risk and vulnerability assessments at various 
segments of agricultural value chains (AVCs) of the selected products, including 
collecting, reviewing, and summarizing relevant scientific data for project location, 
developing median and more extreme scenarios for the key climatic parameters 
and associated climate-related hazards (using the most current and credible 
climate projections available); (iii) work with other consultants to incorporate 
climate proofing, and where possible, disaster risk reduction measures in the 
design of the selected infrastructure subprojects; (iv) identify options for reducing 
greenhouse gases in design of infrastructure subprojects under the project; (v) for 
each of the AVCs of the selected products, provide guidance for the assessment 
of the climate change vulnerabilities in respect of increased incidence of drought 
and flooding and recommend mitigation measures that can be applied by farmers 
organizations and value chain operators; (vi) quantify climate financing amount for 
both mitigation and adaptation based on Guidance Note on Counting Climate 
Finance at ADB; (vii) prepare inputs to (a) the Report and Recommendation of the 
President to the Board of Directors (RRP) (b) relevant sections in Project 
Administration Manual, and (c) Gender Actin Plan; and prepare (a) Initial 
Environmental Examination (IEE) with environmental management plan (EMP), (b) 
Financial Intermediary: Environmental and Social Management System 
Arrangement (for the FIL below, through cooperation with social specialists), and 
(c) CRVA (a standalone document different from the IEE. Outline of the report will 
be provided to the selected consulting firm); (viii) assist ADB project team to 
respond to comments received during ADB internal review on the deliverables 
produced above, and (ix) finalize the deliverables by incorporating and addressing 
the comments received. Draft IEE/EMP should be prepared and disclosed at least 
two weeks before public consultations to allow the public and other stakeholders 
to access. The IEE report shall document in detail the consultation dates, places, 
topics, method, number of participants, their composition by age, gender, 
profession, education level, ethnicity, etc. as evidence to show the 
representativeness. 
 
For the financial intermediation loan (FIL) component, the consultant will cooperate 
closely with the social safeguard specialists and conduct the following tasks: (i) 
collect and compile data and information necessary for due diligence of  financial 
intermediaries (FIs) to assess capacity and commitment of each of the FIs to 
manage environmental impacts in accordance with the ADB’s SPS and national

Narrative description of the Project: 
The overall project objective of the envisaged Agri-
business Promotion Unit is to leverage the agri-business 
sector to enhance farmers’ income and the state’s GDP 
substantially. An ABPU has the objective to intensify and 
diversify market led production and improve farmer 
access to markets by promoting sustainable markets 
preferably at local level. As part of the ABPU initiative, it 
is also sought to facilitate preparation of techno-
commercial feasible proposals for FPOs and provide 
incubation services. 
Description of actual services provided 
by your staff within the assignment: 
In all the three states, several direct market linkages and 
credit linkages established with a range of alternate 
processors and retailers. These included large modern 
retailers, 
B2B 
marketing

 Potato: FPCs in West Bengal were linked with ITC 
and PepsiCo as the FPC were struggling to secure 
good variety of potato seeds in the past. With the 
intervention, although the cost of the seed per bag 
was costlier but at the end of the season the 
production from ITC seeds was 50% more vis a vis 
normal seed bags. Thereby the income of the 
famers increased by 10-15% on partnering with ITC 
Technico as input supplier. Moreover, the proposed 
variety has ready linkages with potato chips 
industry. 
 
 Floriculture: One of the FPCs of West Bengal is in 
flower production. The FPC used to sell its produce 
only in nearby markets or the Kolkata market due to 
lack of logistic facilities. After our intervention, the 
FPC bought its own vending cart. It was analysed 
that the cost of transportation reduced by 
approximately 50%-60%, which has effectually 
increased the profit margin. This is in turn helping 
the farmer members realise better prices for their 
produce. The turnover of the FPC in the last 
financial year increased from USD 150,000 to USD 
450,000. 
 
 Banana: The project linked two of its FPC in West 
Bengal with Keventer Agro (a retailer and 
processor) for cultivation of G9 variety along with a 
buyback agreement. Income of the farmers doubled 
through the buyback agreement, since the tissue 
culture once sown yields for three years 
 
 Vegetables: Through market led approach, the 
project successfully linked the FPC to Waycool 
Foods for fresh Fruits & Vegetable (F&V) 
procurement. Waycool Foods is now collecting 
approximately 1-2 mn MT of fresh vegetables like 
tomato, capsicum, cucumber, beans, gourds, okra, 
brinjal etc. on a daily basis from the very doorstep of 
the FPC with complete logistics support. Notably, 
the income from sale of vegetable shows that the 
farmer’s income share used to be around 45% of the 
final consumer rupee and rest was earned by 
intermediaries in the value chain. However, after the 
intervention by the ABPU, the same farmer’s share 
in consumer price increased to around from the 
earlier 45% to 63% by simply cutting down the 
stakeholders in value chain.

identified the Second party as a competent agency to carry out the assignment of
"Promotion and Strengthening of FPOs as Business Start Ups" in the State of Andhra
Pradesh on the basis of experiences, Project implemented in the past technical
competence and assurance from the Second party to actively involved and facilitate
Promotion of FPO in the project.
Whereas the First Party, based on the capacity committed by the Second party has
allotted a target of 30 no. of FPOs in Kurnool, Cuddapah, Krishna, Guntur, West Godavari /
& East Godavari districts of Andhra Pradesh state, for carrying of the work of "Promotion
and Strengthening of FPOs as Business Start Ups"
NOW THEREFORE BOTH THE PARTIES HERE TO HAVE AGREED TO THE
FOLLOWING TERMS AND CONDITIONS
The Promotion and Strengthening of FPOs as Business Start Ups should be
completed within 36 months after srgning agreement i.e., Mobtlization of Producers
Groups/Commodity interest groups for formation of Producer Companies, Training
and capacity building of FPOs, Registration of FPOs, Preparation of Business Plans
of Producer Companies for various activities and also establishment of Common
Facility Centre (CFC) where necessary and for input facilitation, custom hiring, direct
marketing etc. Post-investment incubation services for Agri-business entrepreneurs
should be completed within 36 months The Project period is 36 months.
2. lntroduction:-
Government of Andhra Pradesh has issued AP FPO policy 2016 wide G.O.
Rt No.39B, dated: 02-06-2016 of Agri & Coop (Horti. &Seri.) in order to promote
Agriculture as a viable business entity. The State Government as part of primary
sector mission has formulated strategy for transforming Agriculture and allied
activities into a viable business preposition with ultimate objective of increasing
farmer's income. As part of strategy the State Government has decided to promote
FPOs/FPCs for reduction in the cost of cultivation and increased income through
collective procurements of inputs and produce aggregation for better marketing.
The overall objective will be to increase the contribution of the agri-busin
SS
sector to the State's GDP substantially

To intensify and diversify market led production, improve farmer access to
markets by promoting alternative markets, etc. As part of the initiative it is also sought
to facilitate preparation of techno-commercial feasible proposals for FPOs, agri-
business entrepreneurs provide incubation services to entrepreneurs, organize
investor meets and conferences as well as review business plans of Farmer Producer
Organizations (FPO's)/ Farmer Producer Companies (FPC's).
3. The Summary Deliverables:-
Facilitate Promotion establishment, strengthening and capacity building of 30
Farmer Producer Organizations (also referred to as Producer Companies) with
capabilities for input facilitation (seeds, pesticides, fertilizers); as well as
common facility centers.
Facilitate higher production quality enhancement etc, through infusion of latest
technology and innovative practices.
Facilitate market linkages with processors, consumers and/or with large
retailers and marketers
Facilitate monitoring & handling of FPOs.
Offer other capacity building services
The need for such an investment is apparent from the predominantly small
and marginal size of the agricultural landholdings in the state. Apparently, the
average size of land holdings is barely 3-4 acres per farmer in Andhra Pradesh. This
makes it necessary for the consolidation and aggregation of produce for market
connectivity and prod uctivity enhancement.
4. Benefits to Producers/Farmers and lndustry (Processors and Retailers):
ln quantitative terms, the consultancy firm is expected to contribute towards
increasing the net value accruals realized by producers/farmers by atleast between 50-
100 per cent. Essentially, the benefits to related stakeholders may be viewed in terms
of the following-
For Farmers
Collective buying of Agri. inputs- seeds, fertilizers and p
price/rate
Farm implement bank (custom hiring) at reduced costs.
esticides at discounted

ln addition, start-up's are expected to facilitate value addition to regional
produce and increase employment generation potential and contribution of the agri-
sector to the states GDP.
a. lnterventions:-
Farmers rn the State have been selling the agricultural produce in these
markets. The real challenge lies in organizing small and marginal farmers for
marketing and linking them to high value agriculture. However, to enable the farmer to
fetch better price for agricultural produce, it is necessary to improve the farmers'
access to markets and develop alternate marketing channels. This could be achieved
by: i) formation of farmers groups which will perform the activity of aggregation of their
produce, ii) value addition to the produce, iii) warehouse receipts development, iv)
selling of produce at the rural haats, and, v) providing e- marketing platforms for trade
of agricultural produce. lt is proposed to establish several Farmers Producers'
Company by bringing farmers together in the form of voluntary groups of about 15 to
20 active farmers and federating 20 lo 25 such groups into a Producer Company.
These Producer Companies will be functioning on behalf of member farmers and will
to undertake a range of activities which will result in added

farmers and value to farmers produce. The Producer Company will be located at the
Mandal level and will have a legal status, owned and managed by a Board of Directors
elected from among shareholders of the Producer Company. Generally, the farmers
sell the agricultural produce immediately after harvest for want of storage facilities and
to meet their financial needs. Due to the glut of the produce in the market after the
harvest, typically a farmer may in some cases have to go in for distress sale. A
Producer Company may serve as in input facilitator, custom hiring facilitator or direct
market connector. A Producer company may also establish Common Facility Centres
(CFC) for storage, primary processing and packaging and leverage advantages like
savings in commission agents charges, market fees, other marketing expenses like
hamali, and still be able to link with the value chain substantially reducing the
transactional cost. The Producer Company with Common Facility Centre will in itself
serve as a "private market". Such common facility may be established subject to
availability of access to schemes at Central or State government level.
To form a producer company, producer groups will be mobilized (in some
cases, this initiative may have already been completed by NGO's). lt is envisaged that
an elected committee of members of Producers Groups will form a management
committee and oversee the performance of an incentivized manager/CEO. The
manager will be trained in technical issues of post-harvest management, marketing
and in operating a transparent accounting system.
The consultancy Firm will support the operation of the Producer Company, and
accelerate the cross learning of best practices.
The consultancy Firm would be allowed to operate with a high degree of
independence and flexibility so that they can use innovation and dynamism to develop
a viable PC business plan. The consultancy Firm would exit the project in 36 months
period. The activity over 3 years may be broadly categorised as
i) Social mobilization and formation of PGs, ii) Nurturing and capacity building of PGs,
iii) Formation of PCs or FPO's and establishment of CFCs where necessary and
viable; and iv) provision of incubation services

5.0 Policy and Management
The policy of the formation of groups i.e. Producer Groups (PGs), Common
lnterest Groups (ClGs), Farmers lnterest Group (FlGs) is already in vogue. A PC will
functron within the overall policy and regulatory framework as per the Producer
Company Act. The management of a PC or FPO will vest with the elected Board from
amongst the members. The provision about constitution of managing committee will be
made in the byelaws. The management of PCs will be by an elected Board of
Directors. Therefore, the representatives of farmers will actually oversee and manage
the affairs of a Producer Company or FPO.
The Selected consulting firm shall undertake following:
1 . Firm should submit Bank guarantee for the amount equal to the 10%
financial outlay proposed in the project not less than Rupees Ten Lakhs
after short listing for participation. This bank guarantee shall be
proportionally annual and shall be renewed every year over period project.
2. A full- fledged office and professional staff should be established in Andhra
Pradesh after short listing.
3. Suitable professional staff for the indicated activities with adequate field
experience have to be positioned in the State.
The FPO development approach may be viewed as given below:
CLUSTER IDENTIFICATION - Under this activity, cluster areas are to be selected by
the consultancy firms/agency/service provider in consultation with the respective
State Government departments. However, it should be ensured that a cluster of
8,000-10,000 farmers should be formulated, within one or two mandals, identifying B0
to 120 contiguous villages of a particular district. ln case of plain areas 300

farmers for each FPO and in case of tribal area a minimum of 250 farmers per FPO
should be ensured.
DIAGNOSTIC STUDY - Diagnostic Study is to be conducted by The Consultancy
Firm in the selected cluster area. The Diagnostic Study is conducted to assess the
preliminary situation of the farmers and level of agriculture in the area. The study will
also help in identifying the potential interventions required and understand the
specific project implementation context.
FEASIBILITY ANALYSIS - Feasibility Analysis for the formation of FPO/FPCs
should be carried out by the consultancy firm and then appraised by value chain/
industry. A normal feasibility study should cover aspects such as financial, technical,
legal, political, socio-cultural, environmental, and economic and resource feasibility.
The Feasibility Analysis will establish a case for promotion of PCs in the prevailing
specific regional environmental context of the FPOs.
BUSINESS PLANNING - Business Planning will be carried out by The Consultancy
Firm with the help of selected farmers' representatives. Business planning is a
process through which the strategic and operational orientation of an emerging FPO
is shaped. lnputs to understand the level from which products and services for
farmers' members should be developed, more important will be the collective
visualization of the future of the FPO. Using a variety of tools and systematic
collective reflections, a business plan with proper projections on various aspects
needs to be developed. The key is to develop business plans in detail with at least
10% of FPO farmer members to provide clear vision.
RESOURCE MOBILISATION - Before initiating the operations of an FPO, all
required resources should be mobilized by the consultant with the help of FPO
representatives and Board of Directors. Financial, human (staff), technical and
physical resources should be developed during this particular step. Based on the
business plan The Consultancy Firm should liaise with various financing agencies
and mobilise resources for h iring/pu rchasing and developing various resources.
BUSINESS OPERATIONS - Business operations is the commencement of
procurement, production, processing, marketing and other service activities of a FPO.
The Consultancy Firm should carefully train both the governing and

The entire value-chain related to various agriculture and allied products and
commodities needs to be managed.
FPO DEVELOPMENT MODEL - The Consultancy Firm will ensure that FPO's will
offer a variety of services to its members. This may cover almost all aspects of
cultivataon (from inputs, technical services to processing and marketing). The FPO's
will facilitate linkages between farmers, processors, traders, and retailers to
coordinate supply and demand and to access key business development servrces
such as market information, input supplies, and transport services. Based on the
emerging needs, the FPO's will keep on adding new services from time to time. The
set of services include Business and other services.
An indicative list of services includes:
lnput Supply Services.' The FPO's will channel quality inputs to member farmers. lt
will supply fertilizers, pesticides, seeds, sprayers, pump sets, accessories, pipelines
etc.
Procurement and Packaging Services; The FPO's will procure agriculture produce
from its member farmers; will do the storage, value addition and packaging.
Marketing Services: The FPO's will undertake direct marketing after procurement of
agricultural produce. This will enable members to save in terms of time, transaction
costs, weighment losses, distress sales, price fluctuations, transportation, quality
maintenance etc.
lnsurance Seryices.'The FPO's will channel various insurance like Crop lnsurance,
Electric Motors lnsurance and Life lnsurance.
Technical Services.' FPO's will promote best practices of farming, maintain
marketing information system, diversifying and raising levels of knowledge and skills
in agricultural production and poslharvest processing that adds value to products.
Networking Servrces.' Making channels of information (e.g. about input sourcing,

to the First party for consideration.

All disputes that may arise will be settled by the competent authority, and as far as
possible both the parties will strive to settle disputes amicably through negotiation.

Narrative description of the Project: 
Developing and establishing seventeen cluster based 
industry associations (IAs) with targeted vision and 
committed leadership for the sustainable growth of agro- 
MSMEs in these clusters, with the objective of providing 
various critical services to agro MSMEs in the areas of 
infrastructure creation, marketing, financing and human 
resource development of the industry. Supporting the IAs 
in the development and implementation of Agro Industrial 
Development Plans (AIDPs) that address the cluster level 
obstacles to enterprise growth over the project period; and 
enhancing access of cluster agro MSMEs to needed 
technical and Business Development Services (BDSs) 
including 
entrepreneurship 
development 
training 
programs. Facilitating external linkages & networks that 
will support the IAs to overcome the obstacles that affects 
their competitiveness and implement AIDPs 
Description of actual services provided 
by your staff within the assignment: 
The Government of Assam (GoA), through Govt. of India 
has received a loan of US$ 200 million from World Bank 
for implementation of Assam Agribusiness and Rural 
Transformation Project (APART). Under the ambit of the 
project, the overall objective of Grant Thornton as a 
Cluster Development Technical Agency (CDTA) is to 
contribute towards meeting the overall project objectives 
for establishing cluster based industry associations (IA) 
for sustainable growth of Agro and Horti MSMEs in 17 
clusters by providing services like infrastructure creation, 
marketing, 
finance, 
skill 
development, 
technology

Assisting & facilitating the IA in preparation of

Assist in the opening of a bank account, with

Completing other pre-requisites for registration of

Facilitate in the collection of Registration

laws and instructions for operating a bank 
account 
 
 
Development of Cluster based Agro Industrial 
Development Plans (AIDPs), facilitating funding 
for implementation and initiation of work on 
setting up Common Facilities/CFCs 
 
o 
For each cluster, the SP, will help IAs and its 
members to identify the common needs (like 
common 
infrastructure, 
soft 
interventions, 
training, capacity building etc.) and develop an 
AIDP. 
o 
AIDP would be submitted by IA to DICC. The 
DICC would recommend the AIDP to OPIU (I&C)

under the project. The MoU shall cover the

civil 
structures, 
the 
contractors for the same would be hired by IA (as 
per the procedure prescribed by the project),

o 
The Annual Action Plan (AAP) would be reviewed 
and recommended by the DICC to the OPIU 
(Industries & Commerce Commissionerate), the 
approving authority. The release of project grants 
to the IA for the implementation AAP would be 
based 
standard 
operational 
procedures 
of 
APART and the SP would coordinate with the 
aforementioned agencies towards this. 
o 
The SP will make all efforts for convergence from 
other schemes for development of common 
facilities in the enterprise clusters. 
 
 
Facilitating completion of setting up of Common 
Facilities/Common Facility Centres (CFCs) 
 
o 
For establishment of common facilities/CFCs, the 
Governing Board will take decisions on behalf of 
the IA members regarding setting up and 
management of common facilities/ CFCs. 
o 
The procurement of construction agency, interiors 
of common facilities/ CFCs, like machines, 
equipments etc will follow established commercial 
practices after due approval by the Project and 
adhering the project statutory norms relating to 
Social and Environmental Safeguards. 
o 
The 
SP 
will 
develop 
management 
and 
maintenance guidelines for common facilities/ 
CFCs 
o 
The necessary manpower for running of the 
common facilities/ CFCs will be arranged by the 
IA and SP will facilitate the IA in sourcing of this 
manpower 
o 
SP will arrange special skill trainings for functional 
staff of the common facilities/ CFC 
o 
SP will develop strategies to sustain the CFC 
operations beyond its exit and the project period. 
o 
If already existing common service infrastructure 
in the IA enterprise cluster is required to be 
upgraded/renovated. The upgradation/renovation 
plan for such common infrastructure will be 
prepared by IA with assistance of SP. This will

 
Business Development Services Support to IAs in 
the operation of business enterprises: SP will 
arrange for Business Development Services 
(BDS) Support for IAs. The indicative BDS 
services are provided below 
 
o 
Development of business plans: The SP would 
support the IAs in preparing business plans for 
various 
initiatives 
(including 
common 
facilities/CFCs) 
of 
IAs 
during 
project 
implementation. Business plans would present 
realistic opportunities for IAs to invest in. These 
would specify, investment required, demand-
supply dynamics, market potential, technology to 
be used, its source and sourcing modalities, 
capacity, land requirement and how it would be 
arranged by the IA, return on investment (ROI), 
break even, cost benefit ratio, scenario analysis, 
regulatory and legal aspects involved, These 
business plans would be put upto concerned sub-
office of EDPF where preliminary review of these 
business plans would be done and meritorious 
business plans would be moved to the head office 
of the EDPF for further consideration. 
o 
Introduction 
of 
improved 
technologies into 
enterprise clusters: The SP would support the IAs 
in introducing latest required technologies both 
from public and private sources into the 
enterprise clusters. Innovative and off the shelf 
technologies would be considered for introduction 
into enterprise clusters. Necessary support of 
EDPF in terms of negotiating the technology 
licensing deals would be taken. Also, the IA 
members would be encouraged to attend 
technology dissemination seminars organized by 
EDPF. As far as possible, climate resilient agro 
technologies would only be introduced in the 
enterprise clusters. Efforts would also be made 
for sourcing international technologies that are 
relevant for the state. 
o 
Fostering linkages with input suppliers and 
linkages to debt and investment financing: The 
SP will work out the raw material/ input 
requirement along with specifications for IA. The 
SP would be in close liaison with the FPOs and 
FPO Service Providers and inform them the IA 
raw 
material 
requirement 
along 
with 
specifications so that bulk sourcing strategies 
from FPOs can be worked out. The SP would 
spell out the specifications of raw materials 
required 
by 
IAs 
to 
agencies 
(including 
international) on the production side of the value 
chain, so that only quality raw material with 
desired traits is received by the IAs. The SP would 
also support the IA in external fund raising and 
access to venture investors, business angels, 
bank credit for putting up common facilities’ and 
taking up other joint initiatives. Need based

than a multiplier of the total payments to the Consultant under the Contract for remuneration and reimbursable expenses.

1.
Government
of Assam (GoA), through
the Government
of India has received
a loan of US$200 million from
the World Bank (W.8.) for implementation
of the Assam Agribusiness
and Rural Transformation
Project
(APART). The Project
Development
Objective (PDO) of APART is to "add value and improve
resilience
of
selected
agriculture
value
chains,
focusing
on smallholder
farmers
and agro-entrepreneurs
in targeted
districts of Assam".
2.
The targeted
districts
(undivided
as of 1st April, 2016)
are
Nagaon,
Sonitpur,
Barpeta,
KarbiAnglong,
Kamrup, Dhubri, Golaghat, Kokrajhar,
Lakhimpur,
Darrang,
Cachar, Sivasagar,
[orhat,
Goalpara,
Morigaon
and Nalbari. The project will adopt a value chain and clusters
approach
to achieve the targeted
objectives.
The targeted
value chains are those of (i) cereals
(rice, maize), (ii) pulses
(lentil, pea and blackgram),
(iii)
spices
and condiments
(ginger,
turmeric,
mustard),
(iv) fruits
(banana)
& vegetables,
(v) livestock
&
fisheries (pork, milk, fish), (vi) speciality
commodities
(eri and muga silk). Expected
duration
of the project
is for seven years. For smooth
implementation
of the program
Core Project Implementation
Units (CPIUs)
have
been
notified
in the
administrative
Departments
at
Secretariat
level
and
Operational
Project
Implementation
Units
(0 PIUs)
have
been
notified
at
Commisslonerate/Directorate/
Agency
level.
Operational
modalities
of the Agro Cluster Development
Program would be carried out by Commissionerate
of Industries
and Commerce
at the State
and District
Industries
and Commerce
Centres
(DICCs) at the
district level.
3.
There
are
four
components
to
the
APART.
The
first
component
is
Enabling
Agri
Enterprise
Development,
with sub components
being (i) enhancing
state capacity
to attract
private
investments,
(ii)
setting
up an Agribusiness
Enterprise
Development
and
Promotion
Facility
(EDPF)
(iii) Agribusiness
Investment
Fund (AIF) support
(iv) establishing
sector
stewardship
councils. The second
component
is
Facilitating
Agro Cluster
Development
with subcomponents
being- (i) support
establishment
of cluster
level Industry
Associations
(IAs), (ii) supply
chain
support
(roads,
warehouses,
markets).
The
third
component
is Fostering
Market
Led Production
and
Resilience
Enhancement
with sub components
being
(i) promoting
climate
resilient
technologies
and their
adoption
(ii) facilitating
market
linkages
through
market
intelligence
and product
aggregation
(iii) facilitating
access
to and responsible
use of
financial services. The fourth
component
is project
Management,
Monitoring
and Learning.
4.
The project will achieve the PDO by: (i) promoting
investments
in agri-enterprises,
reducing
the business
and transaction
costs, facilitating
access to finance for agribusiness
entrepreneurs,
and, where appropriate,
push for process, regulatory
and/or
policy change; (ii) supporting
the development
of a modern agri supply
chain; improved
information
communication
technologies
(lCT) based
farm information
and intelligence
services,
and
alternative
marketing
channels;
and
(iii)
improving
producers'
access
to
knowledge,
technologies
and infrastructure
so that they are better able to respond
to market
opportunities
and climate
variability.
5.
APART would support
value addition
in the production
and post-harvest
segments
of selected
agriculture
value-chains;
facilitate
agribusiness
investments
through
inclusive
business
models
that
provide
opportunities
to smallholder
farmers
as well
as stimulate
establishment
of new
small
and
medium
agribusiness
enterprises;
and support
resilience
of agricultural
production
systems
in order
to better
manage increasing
production
and commercial
risks associated
with climate change and marketing
of agro
produce, in the targeted
districts.
The project would adopt a cluster strategy
within the targeted
districts
to
generate
economies
of scale; promote
vertical
and horizontal
links between
local agricultural
enterprises;
enable diffusion of innovations;
leverage
network
externalities;
and channel
public support
for services and
infrastructure.
By adopting
a cluster
approach,
the project would enable all the value chain participants
to
develop competiti
e-mTIt.
ovative products
that meet market demands
rapidly and succesiJfull.

METHODOLOGY

A two tier approach

the State level.

and providing

work-
plans, training
and ongoing technical

Narrative description of the Project: 
The Cold Chain Scheme is aimed primarily at providing 
adequate infrastructure facilities for food processing, 
logistics and cold chain industry across the value chain 
from farm to market. MoFPI, being the nodal agency for 
the scheme, has issued Guidelines for implementation of 
Cold Chain Infrastructure and intends to facilitate 
establishment of Integrated Cold Chain which would be a 
mechanism to bring farmers, processors and retailers 
together and link agricultural production to market to 
maximize value addition, minimize wastage and improved 
logistics and increase in farmers’ income. The Cold Chain 
scheme focuses on sectors like Dairy, Fruits & 
vegetables, Meat, Poultry, Marine, Ready to cook and 
Ready to Eat. 
Description of actual services provided 
by your staff within the assignment: 
Grant Thornton is engaged as Program Management 
Agency to provide assistance for overall program 
implementation. Over a broader prospective, our role is 
to assist MoFPI in appraisal of proposals received for 
establishment of Integrated Cold Chain and to provide 
management, capacity building, co-ordination, review of 
master plans, design and engineering plans, and 
monitoring support to MoFPI in terms of engineering 
standards, 
quality 
requirements

4.1 
Indian Horticulture Scenario 
Globally, India ranks 2nd in terms of total horticulture production, contributing to 11.2% share in world 
production1 following China. In the year 2018-19, India produced 98.5 million MT of fruits and 185.8 million 
MT of vegetables, with total horticulture production of 313.8 million MT cultivated across 25.4 million 
hectares2. India has been bestowed with a wide range of climate and physic-geographical conditions 
which leads to the diversification of horticultural crops such as fruits, vegetables, flowers, nuts, spices 
and plantation crops (coconut, cashew nut and cocoa) across the country.  
India horticulture production is on the rise since the past few years and this has enhanced the production 
area significantly. This increase in production is due to the growing demand of related food by the Indian 
middle class and changing consumption pattern towards fruits and vegetables. According to a horticulture 
report the Indian food demand is likely to grow over by 2.5% year-over-year over the next 10 years3.   
 
 
Figure 1: Fruits & Vegetable Production ('000 MT) 
The major horticulture producing states include Uttar Pradesh, West Bengal, Madhya Pradesh, 
Maharashtra etc. In 2017-18, the total horticulture production was highest in the case of Uttar Pradesh 
(392.48 Lakh Tonnes) followed by West Bengal (324.2 Lakh Tonnes) as may be seen in the following 
figure 3.

Though India’s share in the global market is still 1%, there is increasing acceptance of horticulture produce 
from the country. In the recent years, India has gained significant importance in terms of varietal 
preference and quality of some horticulture produce. During 2018-19, India exported fruits and vegetables 
worth 1,469.33 USD Millions which comprised of fruits worth 692.01 USD Millions and vegetables worth 
777.25 USD Millions in 2018-19. Grapes occupies the premier position in fruit exports followed by Mango, 
pomegranate, Banana, and Oranges, while Onions, Mixed Vegetables, Potatoes, Tomatoes, and Green 
Chilly contribute largely to the vegetable export basket. India's exports of processed food was Rs. 
31111.90 Crores in 2018-19, which including the share of products like Mango Pulp, Processed 
Vegetables, Cucumber and Gherkins (Prepd. & Presvd), Processed Fruits, Juices & Nuts, Pulses, 
Groundnuts, Guargum, Jaggery & Confectionary, Cocoa Products, Cereal Preparations, Alcoholic 
Beverages, Miscellaneous Preparations, and Milled Products.

4.2 
The major destinations for Indian fruits and vegetables are Bangladesh, UAE, Netherland, Nepal, 
Malaysia, UK, Sri Lanka, Oman and Qatar. Apparently, however, Indian export potential is yet to 
be realised and exports are shared more towards smaller markets such as the middle east, than 
the USA and EU for most horticulture commodities. Also, despite being a world leader in 
production its presence in global trade and world exports is marginal. For example, in Banana, 
Indian exports stood at USD 55 million while Ecuadorian exports crossed 2.5 billion in 2018.Our 
understanding about NHB:  
The Government of India set up the National Horticulture Board (NHB) in April 1984. It was set up by the 
Government of India based on recommendations of the "Group on Perishable Agricultural Commodities", 
headed by Dr M. S. Swaminathan, the then Member (Agriculture), Planning Commission, Government of 
India. NHB’s contribution in this direction has been multidimensional: supporting farmers and 
entrepreneurs to face challenges of globalized markets with improved production, post-harvest 
management and value addition through its broad-based entrepreneur-driven innovative schemes since 
the year 1999-2000. The NHB is registered as a Society under the Societies Registration Act 1860, with 
its headquarters at Gurugram. 
The National Horticulture Board envisions integrated development of the horticulture sector by focusing 
on production and productivity improvement of horticulture crops including fruits and vegetables through 
various interventions, penetration of advanced technologies, production of planting material, vegetable 
seed production, coverage of the area with improved cultivars etc. 
Objectives of the NHB: 
The main objectives of the NHB are to facilitate the integrated development of horticulture sector and to 
help in coordinating and, sustaining the production and processing of fruits and vegetables. 
a) Development of hi-tech commercial horticulture in identified belts and make such areas vibrant 
with horticultural activity, which in turn, will act as hubs for the development of horticulture.  
b) Development of modern post-harvest management infrastructure as an integral part of area 
expansion projects or as a common facility for a cluster of projects. 
c) Development of integrated, energy-efficient cold chain infrastructure for fresh horticulture 
produce.  
d) The 
popularization 
of 
identified 
new 
technologies/tools/techniques 
for 
commercialization/adoption, after carrying out technology and need assessment. 
e) Assistance in securing the availability of quality planting material by promoting setting up of scion 
and rootstock banks/mother plant nurseries and carrying out accreditation/rating of horticulture 
nurseries and need-based imports of planting material.  
f) 
Promotion of Farm Mechanization in horticulture through demonstration and its uses at the 
farmers’ field level to reduce labour cost and increase the productivity of horticulture crops. 
g) Promoting long-distance transport solution for bulk movement of horticulture produce through rail 
etc.,  
h) Carrying out studies and surveys to identify constraints, develop short- and long-term strategies 
for the systematic development of horticulture, and provide technical services including advisory 
and consultancy service. 
 
Introduction of NHB schemes and measurable achievements:  
1) Development of commercial horticulture through production and post-harvest management of 
horticulture crops. 
Under this scheme, credit-linked projects for the establishment of commercial production units in a) open 
field as well as b) under protected condition, c) post-harvest management and d) Post-harvest 
management units are eligible for financial assistance as per the NHB scheme guidelines.

Need of Cluster Development Schemes  
Despite high production and market led valuations, fruits and vegetables are known to incur losses during 
distribution from farms to markets. Various studies on the fruits and vegetables sub-section India have 
indicated a loss percentage ranging from about 8% to l8% on account of poor post-harvest management 
and absence of cold chain as well as processing facilities. A gamut of interventions is required in 
horticulture value chains to improve production and productivity, ensure better price realization for farm 
produce, and ensure effective integration into global value chains. Notably, there is also need for a 
synergized approach towards associated interventions, which can be done by focusing via cluster 
development programs. Areas wherein cluster-based approach can be instrumental are -  
 
A cluster level approach will facilitate realizing suitable economies of scale in production and 
post-harvest activities and is required to help realize actual benefit and empowerment of the 
farming community to double their income through the entire value chain.  
 
It will also encourage Farmer Producer Organizations which are an institutional innovation to help 
small holders overcome scale disadvantages and extend their reach to modem technology and 
distant markets. 
 
These clusters could be the pillars on which technical and physical infrastructure may be optimally 
developed with Government participation through PPPs and through SPVs of the private sector 
as well as FPOs.  
 
The approach of developing product specific clusters in different ago climatic zones of the country 
would also help in dealing with various supply side issues viz., soil nutrients management. higher 
productivity, adoption of market-oriented variety of crop in an aggregated manner, deployment of 
good agriculture practices. etc. There is also perhaps a gap in information-sharing mechanisms 
between the State and Centre on SPS and TBT issues.

Development Program of National Horticulture Board 
 Technical Proposal  66 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
4.3 
Our understanding of the Scope of Work of the PMU 
Introduction: The assignment is expected to carry out a series of activities in order to meet overall 
objective of National Horticulture Board, which is - “to improve integrated development of Horticulture 
industry and to help in coordinating, sustaining the production and processing of fruits and 
vegetables.”. The objectives would be achieved by carrying out a series of hard and soft interventions 
under cluster development program through setting up a Project Management unit (PMU).  
The project envisaged to develop more inclusive and competitive horticulture value chains, by focusing 
on small holder farmers and agri-entrepreneurs in selected clusters. The interventions will expand access 
to new and organized markets for producers and enterprises with complementary investments in 
necessary supporting infrastructure, modern supply chain, value addition etc. The project aims to bring 
holistic development to the sector through two major components, which are –  
1. Pilot project for 7 selected horticultural crops covering 12 identified clusters of selected 
crops. 
The list of identified crops and clusters for pilot projects are as below –  
 
 
 
Crop Specific Analysis: Cluster Based Approach 
Maharashtra receives maximum quantum of the assistance by way of subsidy under NHB schemes, 
out of which the major contribution is channelised towards grapes and pomegranate of approximately 
58%. It may be highlighted that grapes and pomegranate are considered as high-value crops which 
result in higher farm incomes. The direction of subsidy into these crops has been catalytic and has 
improved scale of operation, crop management practices and marketing practices among farmers. It 
is also important to highlight that due to such initiatives at present Grapes in one of the most highly 
exported crop from India.
